found a verifier/interpreter mismatch in the Linux BPF subsystem (CVE-2026-31525, CVSS 7.8). arbitrary kernel read/write; become root, escape containers, disable SELinux, read TLS keys out of other processes' memory.  anyway, it starts with the math bars, the absolute value. computers store negative numbers in two's complement. the smallest 32-bit signed integer is -2,147,483,648, and the largest positive is +2,147,483,647. there is no +2,147,483,648, since it simply does not fit. so when you call abs(-2,147,483,648), the C specification thinks about it for a moment, says "undefined," and leaves the room. on x86 and arm64, what you actually get back is -2,147,483,648. you asked for the absolute value of a negative number, you got back the same negative number. thank you computer :D  the BPF interpreter implements signed 32-bit division (BPF_ALU | BPF_DIV/MOD, off == 1, added in ec0e2da95f72) by decomposing it into unsigned division: take abs() of both operands, divide via do_div(), reapply the sign. the handler in ___bpf_prog_run (kernel/bpf/core.c): AX = abs((s32)DST); AX = do_div(AX, abs((s32)SRC)); and look, the kernel even documents this. include/linux/math.h: "the return value is undefined when the input is the minimum value of the type." when DST = 0x80000000 (S32_MIN), abs() tries to negate it. -(-2,147,483,648) overflows s32, the C spec calls it undefined behavior, and the CPU hands back 0x80000000 unchanged. still negative. abs() had one job. this s32 then gets assigned into AX, a u64 BPF register. s32 → u64 sign-extends: 0x80000000 becomes 0xFFFFFFFF80000000. that's 18,446,744,071,562,067,968. you wanted 2,147,483,648, you got 18.4 quintillion; a rounding error of about 18.4 quintillion. do_div() is a 64-by-32-bit unsigned division macro and it operates on this full u64 numerator. the quotient is off by a factor of 2³². the smod path has the same problem since do_div() modifies the dividend in place and returns the remainder, both wrong. 8 call sites across sdiv32/smod32 src/imm handlers, all quietly producing nonsense whenever S32_MIN shows up. the BPF verifier is the safety system that statically analyzes every BPF program before allowing it to run. it exists specifically to guarantee that nothing bad can happen. scalar32_min_max_sdiv() in kernel/bpf/verifier.c tracks value ranges through abstract interpretation. it handles signed division correctly, including S32_MIN. computes tight, mathematically correct bounds. the interpreter, as we've established, computes whatever it feels like. so the verifier thinks register R0 is in range X. the interpreter puts value Y in R0. the safety system and the execution engine disagree about what a program does. in BPF security research, this is where you set down your coffee. concretely: load S32_MIN into R1, load 2 into R2, execute SDIV32 R1 R2. verifier determines R1 ∈ [-1,073,741,824, -1,073,741,824]. interpreter computes do_div(0xFFFFFFFF80000000, 2) = 0x7FFFFFFFC0000000, reapplies the sign, produces a completely unrelated value. use R1 as an index into a BPF map. verifier approves the access, bounds check passes against its calculated range. interpreter uses the actual value. out-of-bounds read/write on a kernel data structure. on every Linux machine running the BPF interpreter. the root cause of all of this: the absolute value function doesn't handle one number. one specific number, out of 4.2 billion possible inputs, and it's the one that gives you kernel read/write. the fix is: c static u32 abs_s32(s32 x) {     return x >= 0 ? (u32)x : -(u32)x; } cast to u32 before negating. -(u32)0x80000000 = 0x80000000 unsigned. correct absolute value, no overflow, no undefined behavior. the kind of function you'd assume already exists somewhere in 30 million lines of kernel code. it did not. I got to write it. :D I reported this, wrote the patch, got it through 5 revisions of review. acked by Yonghong Song and Mykyta Yatsenko. now patched in stable 6.6, 6.12, 6.18, 6.19. if you haven't updated your kernel: maybe do that.

Why Detection Speed Matters in DeFi  In DeFi, the difference between a minor incident and a major exploit is often measured in seconds. Once an attack starts, automated transactions can move funds far faster than any manual response team can react.  That is why detection speed matters. The earlier a protocol recognizes abnormal behavior, the more options it has to contain risk before losses grow. Security is not just about finding threats, but finding them early enough to matter.  Systems like Drosera are built around this idea, continuously monitoring on chain activity so protocols can respond while events are still unfolding, not after the damage is done. 🕵️‍♂️ Lazarus Group is Watching You: How a LinkedIn Message Can Wipe Your Wallet.  Cybersecurity experts are sounding the alarm. The notorious state-sponsored Lazarus Group has launched an incredibly sophisticated social engineering campaign targeting crypto investors, founders, and traders. The Hook: It all starts on LinkedIn. You get a message from a seemingly high-profile VC fund or a legitimate crypto project offering a partnership, a job opportunity, or a seed investment. The profiles look 100% real, often filled with AI-generated corporate content to build trust. The Trap: Once they gain your trust, they move the conversation to Telegram or Discord. Then, they drop a file under the guise of an NDA, a project pitch, or a beta-testing app. Recently, they’ve even been hiding malware inside popular notes apps like Obsidian. The Execution: The moment you open that file or run their "demo" script, an automated cross-platform info-stealer triggers. It targets your browser extensions, instantly grabs your crypto wallet files, exfiltrates session data, and drains your funds within minutes. The Lesson: In Web3, absolute paranoia is your only shield. Never download files from people you met online, don't run corporate "test tasks," and always verify contracts. A friendly recruiter in your DMs might just be a North Korean hacker. Stay alert! 🫡🛡️  #LazarusGroup #CryptoSecurity #Web3 #Phishing #CyberSecurity #Malware

A long long time ago, when I first got into malware, I met a kid who was a little older than me who, by all standards of measurement, was significantly more intelligent and gifted than me.  He made me feel like a moron.  Very quickly he established a reputation on IRC for being "the guy", despite being like, 16. His parents were financially well off and extremely supportive and sent him to DEFCON. He had a really great PC setup. He had it all lined up. He was destined for an amazing and strong career in information security. I was extremely envious of him because he also had a super pretty girlfriend while somehow being a massive nerd. His parents bought him a car. In my eyes he had it all.  On my side, I had some old piece of crap computer. I didn't even have a computer chair, I used some ghetto dining room table chair made from janky wood. It was all beat up and yucky.  I struggled learning C. On IRC I was basically the village idiot and memed all the time (although in good jest). My friend would become frustrated with me because of how slow I learned.  I was a poor kid. I wasn't like, poor-poor like, homeless or whatever, but his parents has significantly more money than mine and were capable for providing for their son in ways my family could not.  I'm not entirely sure what happened because, despite him learning faster, retaining more information, having more resources, having amazing opportunities, ... he threw it away. I have no idea why. He lost his focus somehow and ended up working at a restaurant for a little bit as a server. He later worked at a mall kiosk.  I ended up being the successful one. I ended up having an amazing career in cybersecurity. I ended up knowing far more than him.  Sometimes I reflect on it and it blows my mind. I only surpassed him because I had endurance and was willing to continue the grind.  He had everything on a silver platter. He had so many amazing opportunities. He could have gone so far, he was so incredibly gifted and smart.  I have no idea what he was thinking to make him squander it all.  I guess the moral of the story is that turtle and the rabbit thingy has truth to it.

sample_tweets.text: 북한은 과거에도, 현재에도 지속적인 사이버 공격을 가하고 있다.  이 APT(지능형 지속 위협) 를 통해 장기간에 걸쳐서 타겟을 스캐닝한 뒤 충분한 자료와 시기/방법이 결정되면 실체적 공격으로 감행하는 것이다.  인용 기사를 보면 올해 3월에도 한국의 기업이 공격당했다. 왜냐하면 이 기업이 북한에게 있어서는 고가치의 표적이었기 때문이다.  북한은 자체 생산력이 떨어지는 국가이기 때문에 생존을 위해서는 타국에게 '갈취'해야만 지속이 가능한 국가이다.  이를 위해서 핵을 개발했고, 핵무기보유국의 지위를 원했고, 국경 없는 사이버 공격을 통해 기술을 유출하고, 가상 자산에 대한 탈취를 거리낌 없이 행한다.  대한민국 뿐만이 아니라 전세계에 걸쳐서 사이버 공격을 감행하는 북한의 신뢰도는 공산 국가를 제외하고는 이미 바닥에 있고, 이 실질적인 위협은 국부를 갉아먹는 사태에까지 이르렀다.  이 상황에서 평화 공존의 한반도 통일안을 약속하는 이재명 정권과 정보기관, 그리고 통일부처는 국가의 미래를 북한과 함께 나아가려 하고 있다.  이 엄연한 공격에 대해서 필요한 조치를 적극적으로 취하지 않는 것만 보아도 내국민의 재산과 안전은 우선순위가 밀려났다는 것을 알 수 있다.  도대체 대한민국의 정의는 어디에 있는가? 이 현실에서는 오로지 국민의 각성에 달려 있다..

AN INDIAN DEV IN BERLIN JUST OPEN SOURCED A $100K THREAT INTEL PLATFORM.  Recorded Future charges six figures a year for this. Tenable charges thousands. He put 27 security intelligence tools into one MCP server and shipped it for free.  It's called cve-mcp-server. Built by Mahipal Jangra. MSc Cybersecurity from SRH Berlin. Originally from India.  You plug it into Claude Desktop or Claude Code with one command and your AI agent suddenly has the same vulnerability superpowers a SOC analyst at a Fortune 500 has.  Enterprise threat intel vs cve-mcp-server:  - Price: $100K+/year → $0 - Setup: 90-day procurement → one pip install - Integrations: locked to their dashboard → runs inside Claude - Data: their curated feed → 21 live sources you can audit - Lock-in: forever → MIT-style permissive, your machine, your rules  What it actually does:  → Pulls full CVE details from NVD (CVSS, weaknesses, KEV status) → Checks EPSS exploit probability scores in real time → Verifies CISA KEV membership (is this being exploited in the wild RIGHT NOW) → Hunts GitHub, Exploit-DB, and Nuclei for public PoCs → Maps any CVE to MITRE ATT&CK techniques → Pulls vendor advisories from Microsoft, Red Hat, and Ubuntu in parallel → Scans IPs against AbuseIPDB + GreyNoise → Looks up file hashes in MalwareBazaar + VirusTotal → Identifies malware families via ThreatFox → Scans your requirements.txt or package.json for known CVEs  Here's the wildest part:  It has a confidence label called WEAPONIZED.  If a CVE is in CISA KEV and has an active public exploit, the server flags it as WEAPONIZED before your AI agent writes a single line of code.  Claude reading PRs no longer just says "looks good." It says "this dependency has a weaponized CVE shipping in attacks today."  One honest note: most of the threat intel APIs need free keys (NVD, AbuseIPDB, VirusTotal, Shodan). It's free to register. The tools degrade gracefully if you skip them.  The threat intel industry has been selling you scoreboards. He gave you the scoreboard.  Repo in the first comment.

A Strategic Critique of Israel’s Intelligence Warning: You Sacrificed the First Line of Defense, and Now You Fear the Consequences?  ​The recent warning issued by Israeli intelligence-affiliated sources regarding "red flags" in Syria and the threat of the Jolani regime is a textbook self-inflicted crisis and a belated confession to a historic miscalculation. You express grave concern today over fighters singing Hamas’s al-Qassam Brigades' songs near the Golan Heights, yet you conveniently forget who paved the way for these very jihadist groups.  ​The grim footage coming out of Damascus and Aleppo is the direct byproduct of a policy of appeasement, silence, and the prioritization of short-term gains by Israel, the United States, and Europe.  ​Here are the harsh realities that your intelligence apparatus must finally confront:  ​1. Misaddressing the Threat: #Jolani is Nothing Without #Erdogan  ​Hyping up Abu Mohammad al-Jolani as the primary mastermind is a diversion. The reality on the ground is that the Turkish state, military, and intelligence service (MIT) stand entirely behind this movement with full logistical, financial, and military backing. Ankara has organized more than 40 jihadist and Salafist factions to advance its #neo_Ottoman expansionist agenda. #Jolani is merely a pawn on Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s broader geopolitical chessboard.  ​2. #Sacrificing_Rojava: The Fatal Blunder of Israel’s Security Doctrine  ​For the past decade, the Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria (Rojava) and the Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) served as the sturdiest fortress against #Islamic_terrorism (ISIS and al-Qaeda) and #pan_Turkism. When the Turkish military and its proxies invaded #Afrin and #Serekaniye to obliterate this democratic, secular model, Israel and the West looked away, assuming Kurdish suppression was an internal Turkish matter. By allowing Rojava to be weakened, you dismantled your own first defensive trench against NATO-backed (Turkish) jihadism.  ​3. The Next Target: #Israel and the #Mediterranean  ​Now that Turkey believes it has mitigated the Kurdish threat, the next target of its official naval-geopolitical doctrine—"Blue Homeland" (Mavi Vatan)—will inevitably be Israel, #Cyprus, #Greece, and dominance over the Eastern Mediterranean. The leverage Turkey has gained by installing proxy jihadists on Israel’s northern border is far more dangerous than the Assad regime ever was. Because Turkey is a NATO member, deterring its asymmetric threats will be exponentially more complex for Israel.  ​#It_Is_Not_Too_Late: A Blueprint to Rectify a Historic Error  ​To escape this security quagmire, Israel and the West must immediately overhaul their doctrine through two urgent steps:  ☆ ​Unconditional Support for #Rojava: The West and Israel must extend full political, military, and diplomatic recognition to Rojava. It remains the most legitimate, reliable, and secular indigenous ally on Syrian soil, possessing the ideological resilience to withstand the tide of Ankara-backed jihadism.  ☆ ​The Liberation of #Abdullah_Ocalan: It is time for Jerusalem to acknowledge past strategic errors. Israel's role in the late 1990s capture and handover of Abdullah Öcalan to Turkey was a profound blunder that paralyzed the dynamics of peace. Today, international pressure for the release of Abdullah Öcalan and his paradigm of grassroots democracy and multi-ethnic coexistence could deliver the heaviest strategic blow to Erdogan’s ethno-religious fascism.  ​The Bottom Line: Israel’s security cannot be guaranteed by hiding behind alarmed tweets. If you genuinely want to ensure that the warning signs missed before October 7 are not repeated on your northern border, you must end the appeasement of the neo-Ottoman Sultan. Invest in the only real trench of freedom and resistance in the region: the #Kurdish_people and the democratic project of Rojava. The umbilical cord of the new terror wave in Syria is severed in #Ankara, not Damascus.

sample_tweets.text: 🔓 A VS Code extension just cost GitHub 55 GB of source code.  GitHub confirmed this week that a threat actor stole roughly 3,800 private repositories including its main codebase and internal secrets-scanning tools after a single employee installed a malicious IDE plugin. The data is now up for auction on a cybercrime forum. Bids were at $95,000 as of this writing.  What makes this worth writing about isn't just the scale of what was taken. It's how ordinary the attack vector is.  Developer laptops have always been a bit of a blind spot. Engineers install things constantly extensions, packages, plugins and the marketplaces they pull from don't get anywhere near the same scrutiny as production systems. GitHub is one of the most security-conscious companies in the industry. If it can happen there, it can happen anywhere.  The actor behind this breach has also been connected to malicious Python packages. Same playbook, different tool. The target isn't your servers it's the person writing the code.  This is a good moment to ask: does your organization have visibility into what's installed on your developers' machines?  More in the blog post → threatmon.io/githubs-source-… #CyberSecurity #GitHub #SupplyChain #InfoSec #ThreatMon #Python

🚨 Threat intelligence has uncovered a cybercrime forum post in which an individual is attempting to purchase sensitive customer data belonging to high-net-worth clients from three of Japan's largest financial institutions. The targeted banks are Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG), Mizuho Financial Group, and Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation (SMBC)—collectively known as Japan's "Big Three" mega-banks.  According to the forum post, the individual's technicians attempted to breach the banks' digital infrastructure to extract the data but were successfully blocked by Akamai Web Application Firewall (WAF) protections. Unable to bypass the security measures, the individual is now seeking a third party to obtain the data on their behalf and is actively asking for pricing and offers.  The three targeted institutions represent a massive portion of Japan's financial ecosystem and hold trillions of dollars in assets globally. MUFG is one of the world's top 10 bank holding companies, with over 140,000 employees and approximately $2.7 trillion in total assets. Mizuho Financial Group manages around $2 trillion in assets with a workforce exceeding 60,000, while the SMBC Group oversees more than $1.73 trillion in assets with over 100,000 employees across its global operations.  Security analysts note that attempts to purchase bulk financial data, particularly from high-net-worth individuals and corporate executives, are a hallmark of advanced data brokerage operations. The exposure of such data could lead to devastating financial fraud, corporate espionage, and highly targeted social engineering attacks.   While the deployment of Akamai WAF has successfully thwarted the initial intrusion attempts, highlighting the effectiveness of robust perimeter defenses for critical financial infrastructure, the ongoing demand for this data on cybercrime forums underscores the persistent and lucrative threat actors pose to the global banking sector.

🚨 A threat actor has appeared on underground forums offering a comprehensive dataset allegedly sourced from MercadoPago Argentina (mercadopago.com.ar) for $1,100. The listing, posted on May 29, 2026, details a highly organized database split into three primary segments: Customer Contacts, Payment Accounts, and Transaction History.  The dataset purportedly contains sensitive personally identifiable information (PII) and financial data belonging to MercadoPago users in Argentina. According to the vendor's description, the "Customer Contacts" section includes CRM data such as full names, email addresses, phone numbers, usernames, password hashes, and account status flags. The "Payment Accounts" segment allegedly holds national identification numbers, KYC completion status, account balances, default currencies, and customer risk scores. Additionally, the "Transaction History" logs reportedly detail transaction amounts, currencies, IP addresses, device types, billing addresses, and fraud/chargeback flags.  MercadoPago is the fintech and payments arm of MercadoLibre, widely recognized as the largest e-commerce and financial technology platform in Latin America. In Argentina, the company is deeply entrenched in the digital economy, serving tens of millions of consumers and merchants for both online and point-of-sale transactions. A breach of this magnitude would represent a severe violation of customer privacy and Argentina's data protection regulations, exposing users to potential identity theft, financial fraud, and targeted phishing campaigns.  The vendor, operating under the Telegram handle @funternetwork, has listed sample download links via Gofile and is accepting payments through forum escrow or trusted middlemen. Cybersecurity experts recommend that MercadoPago immediately investigate the authenticity of the leak, rotate compromised credentials, and notify affected users and relevant financial authorities.

🚨 A massive dataset allegedly originating from Argentina’s Poder Judicial de la Nación (PJN) has surfaced on underground forums, exposing 563,000 records containing highly sensitive personal and legal information. The leak, which is currently being offered for sale, includes national ID numbers (DNI), home addresses, phone numbers, dates of birth, and detailed records of legal case clients and support tickets.  According to the forum post, the compromised data is divided into three primary categories: Contacts, Legal Case Clients, and Legal Support Tickets. The records contain extensive personally identifiable information (PII), including national_id, home_address, personal_email, mobile_phone, date_of_birth, marital_status, and gender. The legal client section further exposes case statuses, assigned lawyers, court names, hearing dates, confidentiality flags, and social security numbers. Support ticket data reveals user roles, issue descriptions, escalation levels, and internal comments.  The dataset was advertised by an unknown threat actor using the handle @funternetwork on Telegram, with download links hosted on gofile.io. The leak is priced at $1,000, with the seller offering forum escrow or trusted middleman services to facilitate the transaction. The post claims the data is "fresh and organized," targeting researchers, threat actors, or individuals seeking insights into Argentina’s judicial operations.  The Poder Judicial de la Nación (National Judicial Power) is Argentina’s highest judicial authority, responsible for overseeing the country’s court system, judges, prosecutors, and legal proceedings. Its domain, pjn.gov.ar, serves as the official portal for judicial information and services. A breach of this scale from a national judiciary represents a significant compromise of government infrastructure and citizen trust.  The exposure of 563,000 records containing DNI numbers, personal addresses, and legal case details poses severe risks. Threat actors could leverage this data for identity theft, targeted phishing, doxxing, or blackmail. The inclusion of case statuses, court names, and confidentiality flags may also compromise ongoing legal proceedings, potentially endangering witnesses, defendants, or vulnerable individuals involved in sensitive litigation. Legal and privacy experts warn that such breaches often precede large-scale financial fraud and harassment campaigns.  As of now, the PJN has not issued an official statement regarding the leak. Cybersecurity researchers are monitoring underground channels for further developments, while affected individuals are advised to monitor their financial accounts and remain vigilant against suspicious communications.

🚨 A significant data breach involving Sankuai (Meituan), one of China's largest technology conglomerates, has been reported on the dark web. Threat actors are currently marketing a comprehensive dataset containing user contact details, order history, and support tickets.  The data dump, which is being offered for $1,200 via Telegram and dark web forums, claims to originate from Sankuai's internal systems. The dataset is structured into three main categories: Contacts, Order History, and Support Tickets.  **Data Compromised**  According to the threat intelligence report, the compromised data includes highly sensitive Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and authentication credentials:  *   **Contacts:** Usernames, full names, email addresses, mobile phone numbers, registration dates, and avatar URLs. The headers also reference legacy messenger IDs (MSN, AIM, ICQ), suggesting the data may be mapped to a specific database schema. *   **Order History:** Detailed transaction records including order totals, payment methods, shipping and billing addresses, tracking numbers, and discount codes. *   **Support Tickets:** Critical security data such as password hashes, password reset tokens, and private messages between users and support staff.  **About Sankuai**  Sankuai, commonly known as Meituan, is a Fortune 500 company and the largest food delivery service in China. Formed by the merger of Meituan and Dianping, the conglomerate operates in sectors including travel, hotel booking, entertainment, and daily necessities. The company lists on NASDAQ and the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, with a market capitalization historically valued in the tens of billions of dollars.  The exposure of billing addresses and password hashes poses a severe risk to users, potentially facilitating identity theft, financial fraud, and credential stuffing attacks.  **Marketplace Details**  The data is being distributed through file-hosting services like gofile.io. The sellers have indicated that secure transactions via forum escrow or trusted middlemen are accepted. The breach report was timestamped May 29, 2026.

Hello,  If you're a person who enjoys malware and/or knows Python and wants to see malware that targets STEAM and GAMERS, I have the source code to a malware I have named "Stealer.Python.GMBA.Manipulator".  This malware was originally noted on Xitter from @GMBA.  In summary, this Python malware kills the Steam process and relaunches it with the "-cef-enable-debugging" flag. Because Steam is a Chromium app, this allows the malware payload to manipulate Steam web pages with web socket gunk and Javascript gunk.  This malware can "modify" user inventories, "block users", etc. It is all a facade designed to trick and social engineer Steam users into giving their expensive Counter Strike stuff to them.  It appears to be written using AI. Regardless of that fact this malware is creative and I like it.  The malware source code to this can be found under the "/Python/" directory. It is named "Stealer.Python.GMBA.Manipulator.7z".  This malware campaign is still active and the C2 is still live. If you execute the __main__.py file you might cook yourself, so be careful. Alternatively, you can run this in a VM and send the malware campaign authors pictures of Goatse.  github.com/vxunderground/Mal…

I learned quite a bit from this actually.  I didn't know Steam was a Chromium app. Hence, you can kill Steam then relaunch it with the "-cef-enable-debugging" flag.  Once you'll launched Steam with this, you can inject Javascript into Steam using Chromium "webSocketDebuggingUrl" stuff.  This malware has a whole pseudo-framework of Javascript that can do: - Alert Bell (?) - Block pages - "Help page" (?) - Inventory manipulation - Steam library manipulation - Profile manipulation - Steam redirections  Basically, this malware payload switches Steam into a Chromium debug state, then sends web debug requests (kind of like Chrome Dev Tools?) to manipulate the Steam pages. It injects Javascript.  The chat window that spawns is from a remote host they control. This is really cool.  Is it AI slop? Yes  Is this code EXTREMELY easy to reverse engineer? Yes  Did they unironically document their entire code base in Russian because it was (probably) written using Claude and the authors probably speak Russian? Yes  Is this extremely creative and cool? Yes  Special thanks to "pro" from 2c44. He handed me the payload and the decompiled Python. The malware .py was Base64 encoded ... so obtaining the original source was ridiculously easy.

Hello,  Awhile ago some guy on Xitter was talking about his friend being scammed and losing Counter Strike stuff. I'm not a gamer, I don't understand Counter Strike markets and stuff, but the gist of everything was he purchased an item and he was (in some capacity) scammed?  He said Steam support was DMing him over Steam. People were memeing him, saying Steam doesn't communicate over Steam like an instant messenger client. People questioned the validity of the images.  I had a bunch of people DM me, tag me on the post, etc. I saw it, but I was busy with my baby boy, so I put it on the back burner. However, it peaked my interest because it was extremely unusual. I do play stuff on Steam sometimes, and I've never seen or heard of malware which is curated to specifically target Steam coupled with social engineering work.  Two things  1. I get tons of messages, DMs, and emails. I can't find the original post anymore. If you know what I'm describing please comment it below, or something, I don't know. The post itself is interesting and provides context to second part of this write-up.  2. This is malware. I was on THE STREETS DAWG (talking with stinky nerds on Telegram) passively to see if anyone knew anything about this. I was able to receive the payload as well the decompiled source code (it's written in Python). This malware was developed by some nerds in Russia determined to ... drain people on Counter Strike and steal their items? Again, I'm not a gamer or Counter Strike nerd, so I don't understand the objective of this malware or the monetary value behind this, but apparently it is enough to motivate someone to create malware which injects itself into Steam to allow them to manipulate the application and impersonate Steam support (API hooking).  I haven't had a chance to review the malware in totality yet. I've briefly skimmed it. It's got a bunch of different modules and stages. Someone seems to have put quite a bit of effort into this. I've never seen anything like this, so it's really cool.  On a side note, I've been noticing a trend of Threat Actors targeting Steam. It was initially by creating fake and malicious games. Now we are seeing malware payloads that inject themselves into the Steam application itself and manipulate it in ways to trick users into giving them valuable video game items or potentially pushing more malware to their machine.  Very cool.

When I taught gen chem lab, as a grad student, I once had a talented-but-lazy student who earned either a 95+ or a zero on every assignment.   By the last week of the term, his grade was hovering between an F and a C. I emailed him, to say that he would pass if he got his last lab report into my box by 5 PM on Friday.   At 6:30, he emailed back to say that he had just put his lab report in my box. I told him that I'd already gone home, that I wouldn't be back on campus until Monday, and that my grades were due at 8 PM, so he would get a zero.   He failed that class, because he had earned an F, despite only turning in A/A+ work.  Two years later, we happened to be at the same rave (my husband was DJing). He ran over to me, reintroduced himself (I remembered him, of course), gave me a hug, and said, "I just wanted to let you know that I deserved that F. You tried really hard to help me pass that class, and I just wasn't getting with the program."   He went on to tell me about how that F had helped him grow as a person and reevaluate his priorities, and that he was really grateful that I'd stood my ground, because I was the first teacher he'd ever had who didn't just let him skate by. He might have been a little high, but it was a really sweet and meaningful conversation.   He was right: I DID try really hard to help him pass that class. He was a nice kid, and a talented one, and I truly wanted him to succeed. He just needed to put in the work, and he wasn't yet in a place where he was willing to do that.

dge shipped DBSC in v147 (April 2026) same hardware-bound session credentials (TPM on Windows, etc.) as Chrome 146.   It’s live in stable now (v148). Microsoft’s web platform release notes confirm it rolled out after their origin trial ended last October.   Great anti-cookie-theft win across both browsers!

Device-bound cookies just killed easy cookie theft, so HVNC is the new meta.  Attackers run a hidden desktop on the victim machine and control the real session live. No stolen cookies needed anymore.  Endpoint detection & response just became non-negotiable. Great catch!

DBSC is HTTP/app-layer: browser proves possession of TPM/hardware-bound private key to issue/refresh short-lived session cookies. Token Binding was TLS-level and died because proxies, CDNs & TLS termination broke it.

🟡 위협 점수: 5/10 (보통)

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 공격 생태계는 LLM(Large Language Model) 에이전트를 활용한 자동화된 공격과 AI 기반 해킹 도구의 확산이 핵심 트렌드로 자리 잡았습니다. 특히, 공격자들이 AI 모델의 취약점을 이용해 코드 실행을 유도하거나, AI 에이전트를 통해 포스트 익스플로잇(Post-Exploitation) 단계를 자동화하는 사례가 급증하고 있습니다. 또한, PAN-OS와 같은 엔터프라이즈 보안 장비의 인증 우회 취약점이 실시간으로 악용되고 있으며, 공급망 공격과 결합된 복합형 악성코드(Stealer, RAT, Ransomware 통합)의 위협이 지속되고 있습니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
- **Kimsuky (데일리시큐, AhnLab ASEC):** 한국 군·기업 관계자를 대상으로 보안 프로그램 및 화상회의 서비스 사칭 공격 수행.
- **UNC6671 (Mandiant):** 'BlackFile'이라는 명칭의 비싱(Vishing) 및 갈취 캠페인 운영.
- **Screening Serpens (Unit42):** AppDomainManager Hijacking 및 신규 RAT 변종을 사용하여 기술·방산 분야 타겟팅.
- **ShinyHunters (Malwarebytes):** Carnival 데이터 유출 사건의 배후로 지목됨.
- **Handala (트위터/X):** 홀로코스트 피해자 지원 센터 대상 데이터 유출 및 사이버 공격 수행.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
- **Kimsuky 활동:** 3~4월 한국 군·기업 관계자를 겨냥한 정교한 피싱 공격(JSONPing, Webex 사칭) 포착.
- **카드사 사칭:** 국내 유명 카드사 보안 메일로 위장한 악성 LNK 파일 유포 정황 확인.
- **기타:** 일본 정부 기관 및 교육 기업 데이터 유출 사건이 국내 보안 커뮤니티에서 주요 이슈로 다뤄짐.

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
- **CVE-2026-31525:** Linux BPF subsystem, CVSS 7.8, 권한 상승 및 컨테이너 탈출 가능.
- **CVE-2026-0257:** Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect, CVSS 7.8, **Active Exploitation(실제 악용 중)**.
- **CVE-2026-39987:** Marimo, LLM 에이전트 기반 포스트 익스플로잇에 활용됨.
- **CVE-2026-46333:** Linux kernel ptrace path, 로컬 권한 상승 및 자격 증명 탈취 가능.
- **CVE-2026-45585:** Windows Disk Encryption Bypass (YellowKey), CVSS 6.8.

### 5. IOC 요약
- **CVE:**
    - CVE-2026-0257 ([TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/pan-os-globalprotect-authentication.html), [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/palo-alto-globalprotect-vpn-auth-bypass-flaw-now-exploited-in-attacks/))
    - CVE-2026-39987 ([TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/attackers-use-llm-agent-for-post.html))
    - CVE-2026-46333 ([Qualys Blog](https://blog.qualys.com/category/vulnerabilities-threat-research))

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
- **PAN-OS 업데이트:** GlobalProtect 인증 우회 취약점(CVE-2026-0257)에 대한 벤더사 보안 패치를 즉시 적용하고 VPN 접근 제어 강화.
- **피싱 대응:** 카드사나 보안 프로그램 설치를 유도하는 메일 수신 시, 링크 클릭을 지양하고 공식 홈페이지를 통해 직접 확인.
- **AI 도구 관리:** 사내 개발자가 사용하는 AI 기반 앱 및 섀도 IT(Shadow IT) 현황을 점검하고, 사내 시스템과 연결된 AI 도구의 권한을 최소화.

### 7. 위협 수준 평가
- **점수:** 8.5/10
- **근거:** PAN-OS 인증 우회 취약점이 실제 공격에 활발히 악용되고 있으며, AI 에이전트를 활용한 자동화된 공격 기법이 실무 환경에 위협이 되고 있음.
